claims.claim: Behavior is attached via ten nilable function-valued public fields on Command, not via methods.
claims.rationale: Nilable function fields model optional hooks with zero boilerplate in the common case.
claims.excerpt: Behavior is attached via **ten function-valued public fields** on the struct (`command.go:128-146`)

claims.claim: Cobra's core type must be a concrete struct with public fields, not an interface.
claims.rationale: Struct-oriented shape enables declarative literal construction, which is the dominant idiom in Go and determines usability at scale.
claims.excerpt: Cobra's core type is a concrete `struct Command` declared in `command.go:54-260`. ... There is **no `Commander` interface** anywhere in the codebase.

claims.claim: Cross-cutting middleware must be implemented via PersistentPreRun(E) or by attaching closures at construction; no middleware-wrapping hook exists.
claims.rationale: A consequence of the struct model: no shared interface to hook against.
claims.excerpt: They must either walk the tree programmatically and set `PersistentPreRunE` on the root, or factor middleware into a closure they attach to each command at construction.

claims.claim: Deprecated fields remain on the struct rather than being removed or renamed.
claims.rationale: Renames are breaking changes to every user; deprecation proceeds by addition.
claims.excerpt: deprecations must proceed by adding new fields rather than changing old ones. See the legacy `BashCompletionFunction` field at `command.go:102`, which remains on the struct after the V2 completion system superseded it.

claims.claim: Derived state should be recomputed on access rather than cached, because there is no hook to invalidate caches when users mutate fields.
claims.rationale: Users mutate public fields directly at any time, so cache invalidation has no hook to run on.
claims.excerpt: The safe path is to recompute on every access (as `UsageTemplate()` and friends do via parent-chain recursion). This is *why* the library avoids caching derived state

claims.claim: Dispatch must be a nil check on the field followed by a direct function call, not a method call on an interface value.
claims.rationale: Direct function-field invocation avoids interface vtable overhead and matches the data model.
claims.excerpt: There is no `v.Run(args)` method call on an interface value. There is a nil check on a field, then a direct function call.

claims.claim: Doc generation and completion read struct fields directly rather than going through accessor methods.
claims.rationale: Keeping commands as data makes metadata accessible without bloating an interface contract.
claims.excerpt: `doc.GenMarkdownTree`, `doc.GenManTree`, and the shell-completion generators can walk the command tree and inspect `Short`, `Long`, `Example`, `Flags()`, `ValidArgs` directly.

claims.claim: Evolution of Command must proceed by adding new fields, not changing or hiding existing ones.
claims.rationale: Public fields are part of the semantic contract; renaming or hiding breaks every user.
claims.excerpt: The escape valve is to add new fields (`RunContext`, etc.) alongside, not to move the existing ones.

claims.claim: Internal methods on *Command are permitted; the rule governs only the user-facing command definition shape.
claims.rationale: Clarifies the scope of the constraint — structs with fields constructed literally is what must be preserved.
claims.excerpt: Internal methods on `*Command` are fine and plentiful ... The rule is about the *user-facing shape of the command definition*: structs with fields, constructed literally.

claims.claim: Misconfiguration is detected at runtime, not compile time; users needing stronger guarantees must add their own validation.
claims.rationale: The type system cannot express 'must have Use and either Run or children' under the struct model.
claims.excerpt: Misconfiguration fails at runtime, not compile time. There is no type-level contract ... Users who want stronger guarantees must add their own validation.

claims.claim: Moving field access behind methods, introducing a Commander interface, or adding a registration contract is a breaking redesign, not a refactor.
claims.rationale: Such changes would force every user of cobra to rewrite their command definitions.
claims.excerpt: Any change that moves field access behind methods ... introduces a `Commander` interface ... is a **regression of this ADR** and must be treated as a breaking redesign, not a refactor.

claims.claim: New behavior is added as new sibling fields rather than by modifying existing fields' signatures.
claims.rationale: Preserves backward compatibility given the struct is effectively frozen in shape.
claims.excerpt: it's why cobra has `RunE` as a separate field rather than a parameter on `Run`, and why `SetContext` was added rather than making `ctx` a private field with only a getter

claims.claim: No Commander interface may be introduced for user command types.
claims.rationale: Interface-based dispatch would force users into a second idiom and require optional-method detection via type assertions.
claims.excerpt: There is **no `Commander` interface** anywhere in the codebase.

claims.claim: Registration is direct slice append/mutation via AddCommand/RemoveCommand; no registration hook, factory, or builder.
claims.rationale: Direct struct mutation keeps registration simple and consistent with the data-oriented model.
claims.excerpt: Registration is **slice append**, not interface assertion. ... There is no registration hook, no factory, no builder — direct struct mutation.

claims.claim: Runnability is derived from data (Run != nil || RunE != nil), not declared through a type/interface.
claims.rationale: Availability of behavior is computed from fields, preserving the data-oriented model.
claims.excerpt: `Runnable()` (`command.go:1596-1597`) is literally `return c.Run != nil || c.RunE != nil`. There is no `type Runnable interface`

claims.claim: The command tree is represented as parent pointer and commands slice on Command; all tree operations walk these fields.
claims.rationale: Consistent data representation across all features reasoning about the tree.
claims.excerpt: There is no `type Tree interface`; parent/child is two pointer fields (`parent`, `commands`) and a slice

claims.claim: Framework-level metadata on flags is attached via pflag's Annotations map, with annotation keys defined as constants in one place per feature.
claims.rationale: Annotations are stringly typed; centralizing keys mitigates typo risk and drift.
claims.excerpt: Cobra mitigates this by defining the keys as unexported constants in one place per feature

claims.claim: The Command struct owns five pflag FlagSets: flags, pflags, lflags, iflags, and parentsPflags, each with a specific role.
claims.rationale: Separates full resolved flags, persistent flags, local-only cache, inherited-only cache, and ancestor persistent flags used during merge.
claims.excerpt: The `Command` struct owns four pflag FlagSets (`command.go:155-166`):

claims.claim: Cobra configures pflag with flag.ContinueOnError so flag parsing accumulates errors instead of calling os.Exit inside the library.
claims.rationale: Lets cobra control error handling, produce suggestions, and respect SilenceErrors/SilenceUsage.
claims.excerpt: Cobra configures pflag with `flag.ContinueOnError` (`command.go:1690`) so that flag parsing accumulates errors rather than calling `os.Exit` from inside the library.

claims.claim: Cobra depends on github.com/spf13/pflag exclusively for flag parsing; stdlib flag is not used for internal flag handling.
claims.rationale: pflag provides POSIX long/short flags, annotations, FlagSet merging, and ContinueOnError — all required by cobra's feature set and unavailable in stdlib flag.
claims.excerpt: all cobra flag handling goes through pflag; stdlib `flag` is referenced only as a compatibility bridge at the root-command boundary.

claims.claim: Cobra does not translate pflag error messages; customization requires SetFlagErrorFunc at the cobra layer.
claims.rationale: End-user flag parse error messages come directly from pflag.
claims.excerpt: Cobra does not translate pflag errors; the messages are pflag's.

claims.claim: Cobra uses pflag's POSIX short+long API (e.g., BoolP, StringP) to declare paired short and long flag forms in a single call.
claims.rationale: Matches the kubectl/git CLI style cobra targets; stdlib flag cannot express this pairing.
claims.excerpt: The default version flag is added via `BoolP("version", "v", false, usage)`

claims.claim: Cobra uses pflag's ShorthandLookup to detect short-flag collisions when auto-adding -h and -v.
claims.rationale: Needed because stdlib has no separate shorthand namespace or lookup API.
claims.excerpt: Shorthand lookup is needed for collision detection.

claims.claim: FParseErrWhitelist is a direct alias of pflag's ParseErrorsAllowlist, intentionally leaking a pflag type into cobra's public API.
claims.rationale: Chosen over a wrapper to preserve ergonomic one-line initialization, at the cost of coupling cobra's API to pflag's type.
claims.excerpt: Cobra chose the alias over a wrapper to keep the ergonomic one-line initialization

claims.claim: Flag caches (lflags, iflags, parentsPflags) must be invalidated when the command tree changes via AddCommand/RemoveCommand.
claims.rationale: Changing parent/child pointers without invalidating flag caches causes a child to see a detached parent's flags — a silent correctness bug.
claims.excerpt: changing parent/child pointers without invalidating the flag caches is a silent correctness bug where a child keeps seeing a detached parent's flags.

claims.claim: In files touching flags, pflag must be imported with the alias `flag` (i.e., `import flag "github.com/spf13/pflag"`).
claims.rationale: Consistent aliasing convention across the codebase; stdlib flag is imported only where truly needed.
claims.excerpt: the type is aliased as `flag` in every file that touches flags

claims.claim: New flag features are implemented either upstream in pflag or as pflag-annotation consumers in cobra; cobra does not reimplement flag parsing, swap pflag, or fall back to stdlib flag internally.
claims.rationale: Preserves the layered architecture and keeps pflag reusable for non-cobra consumers like viper.
claims.excerpt: Cobra does not reimplement flag parsing; it does not swap pflag for another library; it does not fall back to stdlib `flag` for any internal use.

claims.claim: Persistent-flag inheritance is implemented by merging ancestor persistent FlagSets into each descendant's FlagSet at resolution/execute time via pflag's AddFlagSet.
claims.rationale: Enables users to declare a flag once on the root and have every subcommand honor it.
claims.excerpt:
c.Flags().AddFlagSet(c.PersistentFlags())
c.Flags().AddFlagSet(c.parentsPflags)

claims.claim: PersistentFlags() and Flags() are separate FlagSets; flags registered on PersistentFlags() only appear in Flags() after the merge during execute().
claims.rationale: Tests or code that Lookup on Flags() before execute will fail silently — a known gotcha.
claims.excerpt: Tests that register persistent flags and then `Lookup` on `Flags()` before execute fail silently.

claims.claim: Stdlib-flag interop via root command's PersistentFlags().AddFlagSet(flag.CommandLine) must remain supported.
claims.rationale: Programs that adopted cobra incrementally depend on this escape hatch to integrate existing stdlib flag declarations.
claims.excerpt: Removing this interop would break a class of real-world programs. It must remain supported even as pflag evolves.

claims.claim: A command defined with only Run (no RunE) cannot signal failure except via os.Exit, which bypasses PostRun, PersistentPostRun, and global finalizers.
claims.rationale: Historical norm but dangerous; documentation nudges new users toward RunE.
claims.excerpt: A command defined with only `Run: func(...) { ... }` has no way to signal failure except `os.Exit(1)` from inside the hook. This bypasses the entire remaining pipeline.

claims.claim: An error from PreRunE skips ValidateRequiredFlags; PreRunE cannot rely on flag validation having occurred.
claims.rationale: Flag validation happens after PreRunE in the pipeline order.
claims.excerpt: A `PreRunE` that returns an error short-circuits the pipeline before flag validation.

claims.claim: By default (EnableTraverseRunHooks=false), only the nearest ancestor with a PersistentPreRun(E) hook fires; the chain walks child-to-root and breaks at the first defined hook.
claims.rationale: Preserves backward compatibility with users who depended on override (not augment) semantics; running all ancestors would double-execute side effects.
claims.excerpt: Walk from child to root. The **first command in the chain with a PersistentPreRun(E)** is the only one that runs.

claims.claim: Callers of rootCmd.Execute() receive only the first error any pipeline step produced; programs needing to know which step failed must wrap errors with context at each hook.
claims.rationale: The pipeline returns up through execute() on first error with no step identification.
claims.excerpt: Programs that want to know "which step failed" must wrap their own errors with context at each hook.

claims.claim: Cleanup code that must run after Run regardless of success must use defer inside RunE, not PostRun/PostRunE.
claims.rationale: PostRun is skipped on RunE failure; this is the most frequent source of production bugs around the hook model.
claims.excerpt: Any code that must run after `Run` regardless of success — closing files, releasing locks, writing audit logs — belongs in a `defer` inside the `RunE` closure, not in `PostRun`/`PostRunE`.

claims.claim: Context is retrieved via cmd.Context() inside hooks, not passed as a parameter; hook signatures are fixed at func(cmd *Command, args []string) [error].
claims.rationale: Changing hook signatures would break all existing user code; consequence of the struct-oriented design (ADR-01).
claims.excerpt: It is *not* passed as a parameter to the hook functions — the hook signatures are fixed at `func(cmd *Command, args []string) [error]`.

claims.claim: EnableTraverseRunHooks is a package-level global, not a per-Command field.
claims.rationale: Mixing traversal modes within a single command tree has no coherent semantics; a global makes descent behavior predictable.
claims.excerpt: The flag is a package-level global, not a per-Command field, because mixing modes in one tree is incoherent.

claims.claim: Global initializers (OnInitialize) and finalizers (OnFinalize) run on every command's execute, unrelated to the Pre/Post/Persistent chain; finalizers run as deferred and fire even on error.
claims.rationale: Designed for program-level concerns like viper-style config loading, not command-specific logic.
claims.excerpt: Global `initializers`/`finalizers` are separate from per-command hooks... they run unconditionally as the first thing after the no-op/help early returns, and as deferred finalizers on exit.

claims.claim: PostRun, PostRunE, and PersistentPostRun do NOT execute if Run/RunE returns an error; any pipeline error short-circuits all subsequent hooks.
claims.rationale: Chosen for simplicity over defer-like semantics; avoids forcing PostRun code to defensively handle partial state.
claims.excerpt: if `RunE` returns an error, `PostRun` and `PersistentPostRun` do **not** execute.

claims.claim: RunE takes absolute precedence over Run: if both are set, Run is never called. The same shadowing applies to every Pre/Post E-variant pair.
claims.rationale: Error-returning variants were added later; precedence preserves old Run-using code while allowing new code to opt into error returns.
claims.excerpt: **RunE takes absolute precedence**: if both `Run` and `RunE` are set, `Run` is never called.

claims.claim: The --help check runs before ValidateArgs, so help output is emitted even with invalid args; no hooks fire for --help or --version.
claims.rationale: Expected UX: help should always display regardless of arg validity.
claims.excerpt: A user typing `myapp subcmd --help --bogus-arg` gets help output, not an arg-validation error, because help check at line 934-936 comes before `ValidateArgs` at 968.

claims.claim: The execute pipeline is a long-linear function; changing any step's order is a breaking change, and new hooks can only be added in places that do not shift existing steps' relative order, with nil-defaults being no-ops.
claims.rationale: User-observable behavior and cobra's own tests depend on exact ordering.
claims.excerpt: Changing any step's order is a breaking change to user-observable behavior... New hooks can be added only in places where they don't shift existing steps' relative order, and their nil-default must be a no-op.

claims.claim: Under default nearest-ancestor-wins, a child's PersistentPreRun silently suppresses ancestor hooks; the child must manually invoke its parent's hook if ancestor behavior is needed.
claims.rationale: Classic override-trap; e.g., root logging hook never fires under a subgroup that defines its own PersistentPreRun.
claims.excerpt: Nearest-ancestor-wins means a child's hook silently suppresses ancestor hooks... The subgroup's hook must remember to invoke its parent's hook manually, or logging goes dark.

claims.claim: ValidateArgs runs before PreRun/PreRunE so that PreRun does not see unvalidated argument shapes.
claims.rationale: PreRun may configure behavior based on arg shape; passing bad args would make PreRun semantics undefined.
claims.excerpt: `ValidateArgs` runs *before* `PreRun` (line 968-970) because `PreRun` is where users might configure behavior based on arg shape

claims.claim: ValidateRequiredFlags and ValidateFlagGroups run after PreRun, enabling PreRunE to set flag defaults programmatically and satisfy required-flag checks.
claims.rationale: PreRun is the last chance to set flag defaults; ordering allows cmd.Flags().Set from PreRunE to satisfy validation.
claims.excerpt: `ValidateRequiredFlags`/`ValidateFlagGroups` run *after* `PreRun` (lines 1007-1012) because `PreRun` is the last chance to set flag defaults programmatically.

claims.claim: When DisableFlagParsing is true, ValidateFlagGroups is skipped (early-returns nil).
claims.rationale: No parsed flags exist to group; but can surprise users who set MarkFlagsMutuallyExclusive and see no validation.
claims.excerpt: The flag-group validator (`flag_groups.go:82-84`) early-returns `nil` if `c.DisableFlagParsing` is true.

claims.claim: When EnableTraverseRunHooks=true, every ancestor's PersistentPreRun runs root-to-leaf, and PersistentPostRun runs leaf-to-root (stack-unwinding order).
claims.rationale: Symmetric open/close setup/teardown semantics for users who need full-chain traversal.
claims.excerpt: **Every ancestor that has a hook runs**, root first... Post hooks execute child first, then ancestors — stack-unwinding order.

claims.claim: Aliases share the same namespace as canonical names; cobra does not validate collisions at registration, and collisions cause findNext to return the first matching command in declaration order.
claims.rationale: Users adding aliases must manually check the whole sibling set.
claims.excerpt: Cobra does not validate this at registration time; collisions cause findNext to return the first matching command in declaration order

claims.claim: Ambiguous prefix matches must return nil rather than picking the shortest, alphabetically first, or first-declared candidate.
claims.rationale: A silent wrong answer is worse than an explicit failure; resolution should fail and produce an unknown-command error.
claims.excerpt: Ambiguity returns nil, not the shortest match... Cobra does not try to pick the "obvious" one, does not prefer the shortest, does not prefer the first declared.

claims.claim: Case insensitivity in name comparison is opt-in via EnableCaseInsensitive and applies to both exact and prefix matching when enabled.
claims.rationale: POSIX defaults to case-sensitive; opt-in supports Windows-style ergonomics.
claims.excerpt: commandNameMatches, honors EnableCaseInsensitive (cobra.go:61-62). Case insensitivity is opt-in, and when enabled applies to both exact and prefix matching.

claims.claim: Command.Find walks the command tree starting from the receiver, consuming positional arguments as subcommand names until no match is found.
claims.rationale: Defines the resolution entry point and core algorithm.
claims.excerpt: Command.Find(args) at command.go:757-779. The method walks the command tree starting from the receiver, consuming positional arguments as subcommand names until no match is found.

claims.claim: EnableCaseInsensitive is global and applies to the entire tree; it has a measurable hot-path cost from lowercasing operands on every match.
claims.rationale: Performance consideration for large command trees.
claims.excerpt: The flag is global, not per-level, so the cost applies everywhere in the tree.

claims.claim: Exact name-or-alias match must win over prefix match: findNext returns immediately on the first exact hit before any prefix matching is considered.
claims.rationale: Prevents surprise when a typed name is both an exact command and a prefix of another.
claims.excerpt: Exact match wins. A single pass checks both the canonical name and the aliases; the first exact hit returns immediately, before any prefix matching.

claims.claim: Levenshtein matching in SuggestionsFor is case-insensitive (ld is called with ignoreCase=true).
claims.rationale: Catches typos regardless of case.
claims.excerpt: SuggestionsFor calls it with true, so matching is case-insensitive.

claims.claim: Overriding Args on the root command with a permissive validator (e.g., ArbitraryArgs) disables the legacyArgs-driven 'unknown command' error path.
claims.rationale: legacyArgs is what produces unknown-command errors with suggestions; replacing it removes the behavior.
claims.excerpt: If you override Args on the root command with a permissive validator like cobra.ArbitraryArgs, you lose "unknown command" behavior at root

claims.claim: Prefix matching is opt-in via the EnablePrefixMatching global, which defaults to false.
claims.rationale: Avoids silent backward-incompatibility when new commands are added that share prefixes.
claims.excerpt: Prefix matching is opt-in. The EnablePrefixMatching global at cobra.go:52-55 defaults to false.

claims.claim: Prefix-based suggestions operate regardless of EnablePrefixMatching; that global controls resolution, not suggestions.
claims.rationale: Suggestions and resolution are independent concerns.
claims.excerpt: Prefix suggestions operate regardless of EnablePrefixMatching — the global controls resolution, not suggestions.

claims.claim: Resolution is strictly depth-first-match-first with no backtracking.
claims.rationale: Command-tree design must avoid names that are both subcommands and plausible positional args.
claims.excerpt: Resolution is strictly depth-first-match-first. There is no backtracking.

claims.claim: SuggestFor is a []string field so a command can be suggested for multiple synonym variants.
claims.rationale: Synonyms come in families (fetch, pull, grab, retrieve).
claims.excerpt: SuggestFor is a list, not a single string, because synonyms come in families.

claims.claim: Suggestion candidates are filtered by IsAvailableCommand, so Hidden and Deprecated commands are excluded—including their SuggestFor entries.
claims.rationale: Filter applies at the candidate level, not per-strategy, which can surprise authors of hidden commands.
claims.excerpt: The IsAvailableCommand() check (command.go:866) filters by Hidden and Deprecated, so suggestions respect those. But SuggestFor entries on a Hidden command are also filtered out

claims.claim: Suggestion computation is O(siblings) per failed resolution; programs with very large command trees should consider DisableSuggestions.
claims.rationale: String allocations and Levenshtein matrices are non-negligible at scale.
claims.excerpt: Programs with very large command trees should consider DisableSuggestions or restrict the suggestion namespace.

claims.claim: Suggestion output is emitted via the error pipeline (mixed with errors), not separated to stderr; programs needing stdout/stderr separation must configure both explicitly.
claims.rationale: Default behavior mixes suggestions and machine output.
claims.excerpt: Programs that expect suggestions on stderr and machine output on stdout must configure both; the default mixes them.

claims.claim: SuggestionsFor applies three independent strategies—Levenshtein distance, case-insensitive prefix, and explicit SuggestFor lists—any of which can promote a command into the suggestion list.
claims.rationale: Each strategy targets a different class of user mistake (typos, truncations, synonyms).
claims.excerpt: applies three independent suggestion strategies, any of which can promote a command into the suggestion list

claims.claim: SuggestionsMinimumDistance defaults to 2 when unset (zero or negative); values <= 0 are treated as unset.
claims.rationale: Distance 1 misses typos; distance 3 over-suggests semantically unrelated commands.
claims.excerpt: Defaults SuggestionsMinimumDistance to 2 if unset... SuggestionsMinimumDistance <= 0 is treated as unset.

claims.claim: Teams enabling EnablePrefixMatching should enforce naming-convention gates in code review to avoid silently breaking users when new prefix-colliding commands are added.
claims.rationale: Adding a command whose name is a prefix of an existing one silently breaks muscle memory.
claims.excerpt: Teams adopting EnablePrefixMatching must enforce a naming-convention gate in code review.

claims.claim: TraverseChildren is per-command rather than global because different subtrees may have incompatible flag conventions.
claims.rationale: Some subcommands need to treat trailing args as opaque to embedded tools.
claims.excerpt: TraverseChildren is per-command, not global, because some trees have incompatible flag conventions at different depths.

claims.claim: When TraverseChildren is true, cobra uses Traverse instead of Find, parsing flags as it descends rather than only at the leaf.
claims.rationale: Allows persistent flags to appear before subcommands in POSIX-friendly ordering.
claims.excerpt: When true, cobra uses Traverse() at command.go:821-860 instead of Find. Traverse parses flags as it descends, not only at the resolved leaf.

claims.claim: findSuggestions must early-return empty when DisableSuggestions is true.
claims.rationale: Allows applications to suppress 'Did you mean' output entirely.
claims.excerpt: Early-returns empty if DisableSuggestions is true (command.go:253-255, command.go:782-784).

claims.claim: stripFlags correctness depends on the flag set being fully declared (including persistent-flag merging) at the time Find runs; contributors must preserve this invariant.
claims.rationale: Otherwise flag values that look like subcommand names would be misinterpreted.
claims.excerpt: The correctness of this depends on the flag set being correctly declared at the time Find runs, which in turn requires persistent-flag merging to have happened. Contributors touching stripFlags or the flag caches must preserve this invariant.

claims.claim: stripFlags treats flag arguments as transparent during descent so positional resolution skips over flags and their values.
claims.rationale: Enables invocations like 'myapp --quiet serve' to resolve correctly.
claims.excerpt: Find calls stripFlags to skip over flag arguments while walking subcommand names.

claims.claim: All four customization layers must use parent-chain inheritance: lookup walks from the command up through parents until a value is found, falling back to default.
claims.rationale: Cascade-from-root semantics is the inheritance contract; changes to it (e.g., child ignoring parent's setting) are regressions.
claims.excerpt: Changes to these lookup semantics — for example, making a child ignore a parent's setting — are regressions of the inheritance contract.

claims.claim: Annotations is application-specific metadata not consumed by cobra except for CommandDisplayNameAnnotation, which overrides DisplayName().
claims.rationale: Decouples cobra's help model from any specific application's metadata needs.
claims.excerpt: Cobra itself consumes one annotation (`CommandDisplayNameAnnotation`); the rest is application surface.

claims.claim: Both Hidden and Deprecated are respected by the doc generators; cobra has no way to distinguish 'show in docs but not help' or vice versa.
claims.rationale: Doc generation walks the command tree using the same visibility flags as help rendering.
claims.excerpt: cobra does not have a "only show in docs, not in help" or vice-versa distinction.

claims.claim: Command groups are pure data (ID, Title struct); commands opt in via the GroupID field with no Grouper interface or registration hook.
claims.rationale: Applies the struct-as-data philosophy (ADR-01) to help organization—no behavior, just a field and a template.
claims.excerpt: Command groups are data, not behavior. A `Group` is a two-field struct (ID, Title). Commands opt in by setting `GroupID`.

claims.claim: DisplayName() can differ from Name() when CommandDisplayNameAnnotation is set; shell completion, help, and resolution use different ones in different places.
claims.rationale: A subtle inconsistency that contributors touching display-name code must understand.
claims.excerpt: Shell completion, help, and resolution use different ones in different places; this is a subtle inconsistency

claims.claim: GroupID is a string and is not validated against registered groups; typos silently drop commands into the ungrouped catch-all section.
claims.rationale: No compile-time or registration-time check exists; projects should add custom validation.
claims.excerpt: There is no compile-time or registration-time check that the referenced group exists.

claims.claim: Hidden excludes a command from help listings while keeping it executable; Deprecated additionally prints its non-empty string at execution as a user-visible notice.
claims.rationale: The two have different semantics: Hidden 'whispers' (don't advertise); Deprecated 'shouts' (advertise going-away).
claims.excerpt: Both hide from help, but deprecated commands shout; hidden commands whisper.

claims.claim: Inheritance must be implemented as getter-based recursion, not snapshot copy-at-set-time, so late changes to parent templates propagate to existing descendants.
claims.rationale: Copy-at-set-time would miss late-added descendants and late-changed templates—a correctness bug.
claims.excerpt: Inheritance is getter-based recursion, not snapshot copy, so that late changes propagate.

claims.claim: Per-command template strings are parsed at set-time via tmpl() and stored as a *tmplFunc closure with access to global templateFuncs.
claims.rationale: Parsing at set-time means template syntax errors surface immediately rather than at render-time.
claims.excerpt: The stored value is a `*tmplFunc` (a template closure), not a raw string, so parsing happens once at set-time.

claims.claim: SetErrPrefix only affects cobra-rendered errors; user-returned errors from RunE must be formatted by the user to match the prefix.
claims.rationale: Prefix applies to cobra's own error rendering, not pre-formatted error strings from user code.
claims.excerpt: Users who want uniform "ERROR:" prefixing on all failures must also format their `RunE` return errors to match.

claims.claim: SetHelpCommand replaces the entire built-in help subcommand, removing cobra's default behavior for help <cmd> and help --all.
claims.rationale: Layer 4 is the nuclear option; users who take this path must reimplement default help behaviors or lose them.
claims.excerpt: Replacing the help command removes cobra's default behavior for `help <cmd>`, `help --all`, and the help flag interactions.

claims.claim: Setting an empty string on a Set*Template method must reset the field to nil so the parent-chain lookup is triggered.
claims.rationale: Empty string is the explicit signal for 'inherit from parent'; the design priority is 'nil means inherit'.
claims.excerpt: Empty string resets to default by setting the field to nil; the getter `UsageTemplate()` then walks up the parent chain.

claims.claim: Template functions are registered globally at the package level via AddTemplateFunc/AddTemplateFuncs, seeding a shared FuncMap available to all templates.
claims.rationale: Layer 1 of the four-layer extension model affects every template everywhere as the lightest-touch extension.
claims.excerpt: Layer 1: Template functions (global). Registered at package level via `AddTemplateFunc(name, fn)` and `AddTemplateFuncs(FuncMap)`

claims.claim: The Command struct's method and field surface (e.g., .Commands, .LocalFlags, .HasAvailableSubCommands) is part of the public template API; renaming is a breaking change.
claims.rationale: Templates reference these names directly; downstream custom templates would break on rename.
claims.excerpt: Renaming any of these methods is a breaking change to every custom template in every downstream project.

claims.claim: Third-party libraries registering template functions should prefix names uniquely to avoid collisions, since registrations share a global namespace and last-write-wins.
claims.rationale: There is no namespace or prefix convention enforced, so collisions silently clobber.
claims.excerpt: Libraries that register template functions should prefix them uniquely (`mylib_color`) to avoid collisions.

claims.claim: Version template and help template are separate fields; consistent branding requires setting both.
claims.rationale: Forgetting one produces an inconsistent brand impression.
claims.excerpt: Users who want the `--version` output to match their custom help branding must set both `SetVersionTemplate` and `SetHelpTemplate`.

claims.claim: When a render function (UsageFunc/HelpFunc) is set, it fully replaces the template machinery; templates are not consulted.
claims.rationale: Mixing render functions with templates would be incoherent; cobra chose 'go or stay' to keep the contract simple.
claims.excerpt: A render function replaces the template machinery entirely... when the render function is set, templates aren't consulted.

claims.claim: When both a render function and a template are set on the same command, the render function wins regardless of the template.
claims.rationale: This precedence inverts user intuition that 'most customized wins'; the template is only reachable via the default function.
claims.excerpt: if `usageFunc` is set, `UsageFunc()` returns it regardless of any template set on the same command.

claims.claim: tmpl() uses template.Must and panics at SetUsageTemplate call-time on malformed templates; tests must exercise every template set-call.
claims.rationale: Silent template bugs are not possible, but crash-at-init is, so tests must cover all template set-calls.
claims.excerpt: A bad template crashes the program at `SetUsageTemplate` call time

claims.claim: All flags in a group must be visible to a single Command; flag groups cannot cross-reference parent and child subcommands.
claims.rationale: Group validation operates on a single Command's flag set after merging persistent flags.
claims.excerpt: Flag groups cannot cross-reference subcommands. All flags in a group must be visible to a single `Command`

claims.claim: Adding a new group type means adding a new annotation key and validator in flag_groups.go, not changing pflag.
claims.rationale: Maintains the architectural separation between pflag (parser) and cobra (group semantics).
claims.excerpt: Adding a new group type (say, "at most N of these") means adding a new annotation key and a new validator in `flag_groups.go`, not changing pflag.

claims.claim: Application-level flag annotation keys must not start with 'cobra_annotation_' to avoid collisions with the cobra-namespaced annotation key space.
claims.rationale: The cobra_annotation_ prefix is reserved for cobra's own metadata.
claims.excerpt: Applications that want their own flag-level metadata must pick keys that don't start with `cobra_annotation_`.

claims.claim: Changes to flag_groups.go without corresponding test updates are a review blocker.
claims.rationale: Annotation serialization logic is subtle; tests catch regressions that code review alone would miss.
claims.excerpt: Changes to `flag_groups.go` without corresponding test updates are a review blocker.

claims.claim: Cobra annotation keys for flag groups are cobra-internal and not exported; downstream code must use MarkFlags* methods to produce group semantics.
claims.rationale: Prevents downstream code from bypassing cobra's group registration APIs.
claims.excerpt: These keys are cobra-internal. They are not exported; downstream code cannot produce group semantics without going through cobra's `MarkFlags*` methods.

claims.claim: Custom group predicates (closures) cannot be stored as annotations because annotations serialize only string data.
claims.rationale: Future custom predicate support would require a parallel per-Command registry, breaking the single-extension-channel design.
claims.excerpt: There is no generic group-predicate system where a user could supply a custom check function — because annotations serialize only string data, closures cannot be stored.

claims.claim: Every MarkFlags* method must call mergePersistentFlags() before flag lookup so persistent flags from ancestors are visible.
claims.rationale: Without the merge, subcommands cannot reference parent persistent flags in groups.
claims.excerpt: This is why every MarkFlags method starts with `mergePersistentFlags()`.

claims.claim: Flag-group constraints are implemented as a cobra-layer feature using pflag annotations, with no changes to pflag itself.
claims.rationale: Keeps pflag generic for non-cobra users while enabling cobra-specific group semantics and completion integration.
claims.excerpt: Flag groups are implemented entirely in `flag_groups.go` (291 lines), with no changes required in pflag.

claims.claim: Flags must be passed in the same order in repeated MarkFlags* calls or they will be treated as separate groups.
claims.rationale: Groups are identified by exact string match of the space-joined flag names; different orderings produce different group identifiers.
claims.excerpt: Two calls that pass the flags in different orders create two different groups (`"a b c"` vs. `"c b a"`). Users must pass flags in the same order every time

claims.claim: Group membership is stored as a []string of space-joined group IDs to allow a flag to belong to multiple groups.
claims.rationale: pflag's annotation model is map[string][]string; encoding allows accumulating multiple group memberships per flag.
claims.excerpt: The annotation stores them as a slice (`["a b c", "a d e"]`). On validation, `processFlagForGroupAnnotation` iterates the slice and updates two separate group statuses.

claims.claim: Group validation uses pflag's Changed bit (explicitly provided) as the source of truth for whether a flag is set, not the flag's value.
claims.rationale: A flag with a non-empty default that the user didn't provide has Changed=false and is treated as not set.
claims.excerpt: A flag with a non-empty default that the user didn't provide has `Changed = false`. Group validation treats that as "not set."

claims.claim: MarkFlags* methods do not accept custom error messages; messages are generated from flag names.
claims.rationale: Programs needing domain-specific messages must intercept and rewrite errors or implement custom PreRun validation.
claims.excerpt: `MarkFlagsMutuallyExclusive` does not accept a custom error message. The message that surfaces to the end user is built from the flag names.

claims.claim: MarkFlags* methods panic on missing flags rather than returning errors.
claims.rationale: Missing flag at registration time is a programmer bug, and panicking fails loudly at init where the bug lives.
claims.excerpt: Iterates each flag name and requires it to exist — **panic** on a missing flag, not a returned error. Missing flag at registration time is a programmer bug.

claims.claim: Required-but-ungrouped flags must be enforced via MarkFlagRequired or custom PreRun logic; group validation does not cover ungrouped flags.
claims.rationale: Group validation only inspects flags participating in declared groups.
claims.excerpt: A flag that should logically be required but isn't in any group is the user's problem to enforce via `MarkFlagRequired` (a separate mechanism) or custom PreRun logic.

claims.claim: Shell completion hides flags that would violate a group constraint by reading the same annotations used for validation.
claims.rationale: Improves UX by removing bad choices from completion menus rather than only erroring after submission.
claims.excerpt: During shell completion, `enforceFlagGroupsForCompletion` uses the same annotations to *hide* flags that would violate a group constraint if added.

claims.claim: ValidateFlagGroups() is called after PreRun and before Run in the execute pipeline.
claims.rationale: PreRun may set flag values programmatically that should count toward group satisfaction; validating earlier would falsely reject those commands.
claims.excerpt: The execute pipeline places `ValidateFlagGroups()` at line 1010, after `PreRun(E)` (1003-1005) and before `Run(E)` (1014-1020).

claims.claim: When DisableFlagParsing is true, ValidateFlagGroups short-circuits and returns nil.
claims.rationale: Commands with raw args have no parsed flags so Changed is never set; validation would produce false failures or trivially pass.
claims.excerpt: Commands that take raw args (e.g., wrapping another tool) have no parsed flags; group validation would always trivially pass, so it's skipped.

claims.claim: ActiveHelp messages are a completion-time channel (via `cobra.AppendActiveHelp`) rendered as informational lines by V2 bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell adapters.
claims.rationale: Surfaces contextual guidance during completion rather than after submission; required shell-adapter support.
claims.excerpt: only V2 bash, zsh, fish, PowerShell surface it.

claims.claim: Annotations drive filename-extension filtering, subdir completion, and required-flag marking for completion.
claims.rationale: Part of the completion API surface for CLI authors.
claims.excerpt: Annotations — `BashCompFilenameExt` (complete filenames with certain extensions), `BashCompSubdirsInDir` (complete subdirs), `BashCompOneRequiredFlag` (marker for required flags).

claims.claim: Completion functions must return both a list of candidates and a `ShellCompDirective`.
claims.rationale: Enables Go code to instruct the shell about trailing space, file fallback, order preservation simultaneously with providing candidates.
claims.excerpt: The completion function returns `(completions []string, directive ShellCompDirective)`.

claims.claim: Completion functions should be fast, side-effect-free, and bounded.
claims.rationale: They run with the user's shell privileges on every TAB press; slow or buggy completion makes the CLI feel broken and is security-sensitive.
claims.excerpt: Completion functions should be side-effect-free and bounded.

claims.claim: ActiveHelp is not supported for bash completion V1.
claims.rationale: V1's architecture cannot render completion descriptions or ActiveHelp messages.
claims.excerpt:
// bash_completionsV2.go disables ActiveHelp for V1:
// "Disable ActiveHelp which is not supported for bash completion v1"

claims.claim: CLIs with expensive completion should implement local caching (e.g., filesystem cache with TTL).
claims.rationale: TAB runs user Go code; network-bound completion functions directly impact perceived latency.
claims.excerpt: CLIs with expensive completion should implement local caching (filesystem cache with TTL).

claims.claim: Cobra installs hidden subcommands `__complete` and `__completeNoDesc` on every root command to serve as the dynamic completion protocol endpoint.
claims.rationale: Shell completion scripts invoke these hidden subcommands; the logic lives in Go rather than the shell script.
claims.excerpt: Cobra installs a hidden subcommand `__complete` (and `__completeNoDesc`) on every root command. The shell completion scripts generated by cobra invoke these.

claims.claim: Generation functions emit shell scripts to a writer; a built-in `completion` subcommand exposes them for end-user installation unless disabled.
claims.rationale: Provides the user-facing mechanism for installing shell completions.
claims.excerpt: `GenBashCompletion(w)`, `GenBashCompletionV2(w, includeDesc)`, `GenZshCompletion(w)`, `GenFishCompletion(w, includeDesc)`, `GenPowerShellCompletion(w)`.

claims.claim: New commands should register appropriate `ValidArgs` or `ValidArgsFunction`; omitting them leaves completion silent for the command.
claims.rationale: Completion coverage depends on every command being wired into the protocol.
claims.excerpt: Code review of new commands should check that `ValidArgs` or `ValidArgsFunction` is appropriate.

claims.claim: Shell adapter scripts must never compute completions themselves; they always delegate to `__complete`.
claims.rationale: Keeping logic in Go means one engine with per-shell adapters; new shells are added by writing a new generator, not new completion logic.
claims.excerpt: The shell script never computes completions; it always delegates to `__complete`. New shells are supported by adding new generators, not by duplicating completion logic.

claims.claim: The hidden completion commands are registered at execute time with `Hidden: true` and `DisableFlagParsing: true`.
claims.rationale: Hidden keeps them out of --help; DisableFlagParsing lets the command receive raw, possibly-incomplete user input.
claims.excerpt: `initCompleteCmd()` in `completions.go` (around lines 231-296) creates the subcommand with `Hidden: true` and `DisableFlagParsing: true`.

claims.claim: The output format of `__complete` is a protocol contract with installed shell scripts and must evolve additively (new directives via opt-in bits, never changing existing ones).
claims.rationale: Installed scripts sit on user systems for years; changing the format breaks them regardless of binary version.
claims.excerpt: Cobra must evolve `__complete`'s output very carefully — introducing new directives via opt-in bits, never changing existing ones.

claims.claim: Two bash generators (V1 and V2) coexist; V1 is retained for bash-3 and back-compat with already-installed scripts.
claims.rationale: Removing V1 would break every already-installed completion script on user systems.
claims.excerpt: V1 stays for bash-3-and-earlier environments and for back-compat with existing sourced scripts.

claims.claim: `CompletionOptions` controls whether the `completion` subcommand is added, whether descriptions are shown, and whether `__complete` is hidden.
claims.rationale: Programs that don't want cobra's built-in completion setup can disable it.
claims.excerpt: Programs that don't want cobra's built-in completion setup can disable it here.

claims.claim: `DisableFlagParsing: true` on `__complete` is required because the user's partially-typed flags may not be syntactically valid yet.
claims.rationale: Letting pflag parse incomplete flags would cause errors; cobra needs the raw args to determine which flag is being completed.
claims.excerpt: `DisableFlagParsing: true` on `__complete` is essential. The args to `__complete` include the user's partially-typed flags, some of which may not be syntactically valid yet

claims.claim: `ShellCompDirectiveError` signals programming errors and suppresses fallback; `ShellCompDirectiveDefault` means fall back to file completion.
claims.rationale: These two directives have subtly different semantics and must not be conflated.
claims.excerpt: subtly different from "no candidates, please fall back to file completion" (`ShellCompDirectiveDefault`).

claims.claim: `ShellCompDirective` is represented as a bitset to combine orthogonal completion behaviors (NoSpace, NoFileComp, FilterFileExt, KeepOrder, Error).
claims.rationale: Completion results are multi-dimensional and cannot be expressed by a single-value enum.
claims.excerpt: `ShellCompDirective` is a bitset (in `completions.go`) with flags like `ShellCompDirectiveNoSpace`, `ShellCompDirectiveNoFileComp`, `ShellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt`, `ShellCompDirectiveKeepOrder`, `ShellCompDirectiveError`.

claims.claim: `ValidArgs` and `ValidArgsFunction` cannot be used together on the same command.
claims.rationale: Mixing would create semantic ambiguity between static and dynamic positional completions.
claims.excerpt: Cannot be used together with `ValidArgs`.

claims.claim: `__complete` skips cobra's normal startup pipeline (Pre/Run/Post) though flag inheritance still runs.
claims.rationale: Keeps the local overhead of TAB invocations small.
claims.excerpt: having `__complete` skip most of cobra's normal startup (flag inheritance runs but none of the Pre/Run/Post pipeline).

claims.claim: `enforceFlagGroupsForCompletion` prunes completion candidates using the same flag-group annotations that validate post-parse; changes to either code path must be kept in sync.
claims.rationale: The annotations do double duty across validation and completion pruning.
claims.excerpt: Contributors changing either code path must remember the other exists.

claims.claim: Cobra does not wrap injected writers in a mutex; concurrent writers are user responsibility.
claims.rationale: io.Writer contract is not concurrency-safe by default.
claims.excerpt: Concurrent writers are user-responsibility... Cobra does not wrap the writer in a mutex.

claims.claim: Error output never falls back to stdout; errWriter's only fallback is os.Stderr.
claims.rationale: Defaulting errors to stdout would corrupt stdout-parsing scripts.
claims.excerpt: Error output never defaults to stdout; that would corrupt stdout-parsing scripts.

claims.claim: Goroutines launched from RunE do not automatically inherit cobra's IO; users must capture cmd.OutOrStdout() outside the goroutine and pass the writer in.
claims.rationale: Goroutines calling fmt.Println hit real stdout, bypassing injection.
claims.excerpt: Goroutines launched from `RunE` do not get cobra's IO by default.

claims.claim: Help output is written through cmd.OutOrStderr() (stderr by convention), so tests capturing help output must use SetErr, not SetOut.
claims.rationale: Help conventionally goes to stderr to avoid polluting stdout-parsing pipelines.
claims.excerpt: the template output goes through `cmd.OutOrStderr()` (help conventionally goes to stderr... Tests that want to capture help output capture via `SetErr`, not `SetOut`

claims.claim: IO getters walk the parent chain: if the current command's writer is nil, ask the parent recursively, falling back to the provided default at the root.
claims.rationale: Parent-chain inheritance means SetOut on root cascades to all subcommands automatically, keeping root as the live source of truth.
claims.excerpt: The parent-chain recursion is the inheritance mechanism: if this command's writer is nil, ask the parent; recurse to root; fall back to the provided default

claims.claim: IO streams are configured per-Command via SetOut/SetErr/SetIn setter methods rather than constructor injection, functional options, or global writers.
claims.rationale: Matches struct-as-data philosophy, avoids global state, and enables parallel-safe testing.
claims.excerpt: Cobra commits to the per-Command-injection model with parent-chain inheritance.

claims.claim: OutOrStdout and OutOrStderr both consult the same outWriter field but differ only in their default fallback (os.Stdout vs os.Stderr).
claims.rationale: Allows cobra to default usage/error output to stderr while still honoring user-configured outWriter.
claims.excerpt: The same `outWriter` field backs both — the difference is only in the fallback when nothing is configured.

claims.claim: Public doc generation functions like GenBashCompletion take an explicit io.Writer parameter rather than using cmd.OutOrStdout().
claims.rationale: Consistent with users passing an explicit writer but creates a minor asymmetry.
claims.excerpt: `GenBashCompletion(w)` and friends take an `io.Writer` directly, not through `cmd.OutOrStdout()`.

claims.claim: SetOut(nil) re-enables parent-chain fallback rather than disabling output; to discard output callers must pass io.Discard explicitly.
claims.rationale: nil means inherit in the walker semantics, so nil cannot be used to disable output.
claims.excerpt: Setters are non-nil-preserving. `SetOut(nil)` sets the field to nil, which re-enables the parent-chain fallback.

claims.claim: SetOutput is a legacy combined setter that sets both outWriter and errWriter to the same writer, kept for backward compatibility.
claims.rationale: Early cobra had only one combined writer; splitting needed to preserve old semantics.
claims.excerpt: A legacy combined setter `SetOutput(output io.Writer)` ... sets both `outWriter` and `errWriter` to the same writer — kept for backward compatibility

claims.claim: The cmd.Print/Println/Printf/PrintErr helpers are the preferred API for user output over calling fmt.Fprintln(cmd.OutOrStdout(), ...) directly.
claims.rationale: Helpers read better and implicitly go through the getter pattern.
claims.excerpt: The `Print`, `Println`, `Printf`, `PrintErr*` helpers exist as convenience wrappers... are the preferred API for user code

claims.claim: User code in RunE must route output through cmd.OutOrStdout(), cmd.ErrOrStderr(), cmd.InOrStdin() rather than os.Stdout/os.Stderr/os.Stdin directly, or output will bypass SetOut and tests will fail to capture it.
claims.rationale: Direct use of os.Stdout bypasses the injection mechanism, breaking test capture.
claims.excerpt: User code that wants its output captured during tests must route through `cmd.OutOrStdout()`, `cmd.ErrOrStderr()`, `cmd.InOrStdin()` — not `os.Stdout`/`os.Stderr`/`os.Stdin` directly.

claims.claim: Writers are resolved lazily at print time via the parent-chain walker, not snapshotted at command-tree construction.
claims.rationale: Allows SetOut after children are added to still affect them, and writers can be swapped mid-execute.
claims.excerpt: Parent-chain walker keeps configuration lazy. Writers are resolved at print time, not at command-tree-construction time.

claims.claim: Adding a new IO stream requires adding a new field, setter, getter, and walker; the pattern is not generic.
claims.rationale: Each stream is hand-built; changing to a generic map-based extension would be a breaking API change.
claims.excerpt: Adding new IO streams (e.g., a separate "progress" channel) requires adding a new field, setter, getter, and walker. The pattern is stable but not generic

claims.claim: Each Command carries three private IO fields (inReader, outWriter, errWriter) that are not directly accessible by users.
claims.rationale: Private fields enforce the nil-means-inherit contract that the getter/walker logic depends on.
claims.excerpt: These are private — users cannot set them directly.

claims.claim: Parent walker is O(depth) per print and results are not cached; each OutOrStdout() call walks the chain.
claims.rationale: Trivial for typical tree depths but a cost for pathological depths.
claims.excerpt: Parent walker is O(depth) per print. Every `cmd.OutOrStdout()` walks from the command to root... Cobra does not cache the resolved writer

claims.claim: Tests should use a fresh rootCmd per test rather than reusing the same Command across subtests, to avoid stale writer state.
claims.rationale: Reused commands retain prior writer configuration, risking test pollution.
claims.excerpt: The usual pattern is a fresh `rootCmd` per test.

git_commits.message: chore: Migrate from gopkg.in/yaml.v3 to go.yaml.in/yaml/v3 (#2336)
refactor: replace several vars with consts (#2328)
refactor: change minUsagePadding from var to const (#2325)
Add documentation for repeated flags functionality (#2316)
Add Periscope to the list of projects using Cobra (#2299)
The default ShellCompDirective can be customized for a command and its subcommands (#2238)
refactor: apply golangci-lint autofixes, work around false positives
chore: Rm GitHub Action PR size labeler (#2256)
Flow context to command in SetHelpFunc (#2241)
Revert "Make detection for test-binary more universal (#2173)" (#2235)
Remove the default "completion" cmd if it is alone (#1559)
Allow to reset the templates to the default (#2229)
fix(bash): nounset unbound file filter variable on empty extension (#2228)
Print ActiveHelp for bash along other completions (#2076)
Allow linker to perform deadcode elimination for program using Cobra (#1956)
Add similar whitespace escape logic to bash v2 completions than in other completions (#1743)
feat: add CompletionFunc type to help with completions (#2220)
chore: fix function name in comment (#2216)
completion: improve detection for flags that accept multiple values (#2210)
Make detection for test-binary more universal (#2173)
Make Powershell completion script work in constrained mode (#2196)
doc: azion project added to the list of CLIs that use cobra (#2198)
Fix --version help and output for plugins (#2180)
fix(completions): Complete map flags multiple times (#2174)
Address golangci-lint deprecation warnings, enable some more linters (#2152)
Add Incus to the list of projects using Cobra (#2118)
Add Taikun CloudWorks to list of projects (#2098)
flags: clarify documentation that LocalFlags related function do not modify the state (#2064)
Add tests for funcs in cobra.go (#2094)
Add env variable to suppress completion descriptions on create (#1938)
Add LXC to the list of projects using cobra (#2071)
Fix help text for runnable plugin command
Document how to create a plugin
Support usage as plugin for tools like kubectl (#2018)
Improve API to get flag completion function (#2063)
Replace all non-alphanumerics in active help env var program prefix (#1940)
Add tests for flag completion registration (#2053)
Don't complete --help flag when flag parsing disabled (#2061)
Allow running persistent run hooks of all parents (#2044)
Add notes to doc on preRun and postRun condition (#2041)
feat: add getters for flag completions (#1943)
command: temporarily disable G602 due to securego/gosec#1005 (#2022)
golangci: enable 'unused' and disable deprecated replaced by it (#1983)
powershell: escape variable with curly brackets (#1960)
Update main image to better handle dark background (#1883)
Removes stale bot from GitHub action (#1908)
Improve MarkFlagsMutuallyExclusive example in User Guide (#1904)
Add documentation about disabling completion descriptions (#1901)
completions: do not detect arguments with dash as 2nd char as flag (#1817)
fix: func name in doc strings (#1885)
fix: conflict import name with variable (#1879)
fix: force ForEach-Object to return array in pwsh completion (#1850)
Create unit test illustrating unknown flag bug (#1854)
fix: don't remove flag value that matches subcommand name (#1781)
Add Sia to projects using Cobra (#1844)
Check for group presence after full initialization (#1839)
Replace deprecated ExactValidArgs with MatchAll in user_guide and shell_completions example (#1836)
Add Constellation to projects using cobra. (#1829)
Add groups for commands in help (#1003)
Add allero to list of projects using cobra (#1819)
Allow user to add completion for powershell alias (#1621)
ci: add minimum GitHub token permissions for workflows (#1792)
Document option to hide the default completion cmd (#1779)
Deprecate ExactValidArgs() and test combinations of args validators (#1643)
Use correct stale action `exempt-` yaml keys (#1800)
With go 1.18, we must use go install for a binary (#1726)
fix: show flags that shadow parent persistent flag in child help (#1776)
fix: correct command path in see_also for YAML doc (#1771)
docs: add zitadel to the list (#1772)
doc: Add Okteto for projects using Cobra (#1759)
Add Cilium to projects using Cobra (#1745)
doc: Add CloudQuery for projects using Cobra (#1742)
Add KubeVirt as a project using cobra (#1741)
use errors.Is() to check for errors (#1730)
undefined or nil Args default to ArbitraryArgs (#1612)
Update shell completion to respect flag groups (#1659)
Add unit test for fish completion (#1515)
Fix handling of descriptions for bash v3 (#1735)
Modify brew prefix path in macOS system (#1719)
Add Pulumi as a project using cobra (#1720)
perf(bash-v2): read directly to COMPREPLY on descriptionless short circuit (#1700)
fix(bash-v2): skip empty completions when filtering descriptions (#1691)
Change links from http:// to https:// (#1695)
perf(bash-v2): speed up filtering menu-complete descriptions (#1692)
perf(bash-v2): speed up filtering entries with descriptions (#1689)
style(bash): out is not an array variable, do not refer to it as such (#1684)
style(bash-v2): out is not an array variable, do not refer to it as such (#1681)
perf(bash-v2): use backslash escape string expansion for tab (#1682)
Add Polygon Edge as a project using Cobra (#1672)
Add ability to mark flags as required or exclusive as a group (#1654)
Changed branch name in the cobra generator link to 'main' (#1645)
feat: Add how to load completions in your current zsh session (#1608)
Remove CHANGELOG.md as it isn't updated (#1634)
Removes viper dependency by removing cobra/ CLI tool (#1604)
Update Go Doc link and badge (#1593)
Bump license year to 2022 in golden files (#1575)
Remove __complete cmd for program without subcmds (#1563)
Always include the os package import when generating the root command (#1557)
Fix invalid shell completion when used with ~/.cobra.yaml (#1510)
fix: Duplicate error message from cobra init boilerplates (#1463)
fix: unbound variables in bash completion (#1321)
Update shell_completions.md to have automatically rootcmd (#1372)
